cards.text: A {{c1::Boolean}} variable can only hold one of two values: {{c2::true}} or {{c3::false}}.
Boolean variables are declared using the {{c1::bool}} keyword.

cards.question: How do you declare a boolean variable?
cards.code:
bool isHappy{ true };
bool isSad{ false };
cards.answer: Use bool keyword with true or false
cards.explanation: Boolean variables use the bool type and can be initialized to true or false.

cards.front: What does std::cout print for boolean values by default?

cards.text: To print boolean values as 'true' or 'false' instead of 1 or 0, use {{c1::std::boolalpha}}.

cards.question: What does this print?
cards.code:
bool b{ true };
std::cout << std::boolalpha << b;
cards.explanation: std::boolalpha manipulator makes cout print 'true' instead of '1'.

cards.text: When converted to an integer, true becomes {{c1::1}} and false becomes {{c2::0}}.

cards.question: What value does b hold?
cards.explanation: Any non-zero integer converts to true when assigned to bool. Only 0 converts to false.

cards.front: How much memory does a bool typically use?
cards.back: 1 byte (8 bits), even though it only needs 1 bit, due to memory addressing constraints.

cards.text: When inputting boolean values with std::cin, use {{c1::std::boolalpha}} to allow 'true' and 'false' text input instead of just 0 and 1.

cards.explanation:
Use bool for:
  • Flags and state (is valid, is open, has data)
  • Function return values indicating success/failure
  • Conditional logic
Makes code self-documenting compared to using integers.
examples: bool isValid{ true };    // good: clear intent
